<!-- question-type: prepare -->
### Exercise 3: What Is K-Means Clustering?

In Exercise 2, you created a simple rule-of-thumb segmentation by assigning employees to groups using fixed cutoffs.

In later exercises, you will explore a different approach called **K-means clustering**.

**(a)** Explain in your own words what K-means clustering is trying to achieve.

**(b)** Explain how K-means clustering differs from the rule-of-thumb segmentation you created in Exercise 2.

**(c)** What is one advantage of using a rule-of-thumb segmentation instead of K-means clustering?

**(d)** What is one advantage of using K-means clustering instead of a rule-of-thumb segmentation?

**(e)** In K-means clustering, which parts of the analysis are chosen by the analyst, and which parts are carried out automatically by the algorithm?

**(f)** Suppose an analytics team wants to use K-means clustering with variables such as `job_satisfaction`, `distance_from_home`, and `monthly_income`. Explain why it is important that these variables are numeric.

**(g)** Explain why it is usually important to scale variables before running K-means clustering.

*Write your answer here*

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.teaching-block}

::: {.teaching-block-header}
Teaching Note
:::

::: {.teaching-block-body}

🎯 **Learning Objective**

Students should:

* Explain in plain language what K-means clustering is trying to do.
* Distinguish between a hand-built rule-of-thumb segmentation and a data-driven clustering approach.
* Understand what the analyst chooses in a K-means workflow, what the algorithm does automatically, why K-means requires numeric inputs, and why scaling matters.
* Begin to see that clustering involves analyst choices, even when it is more data-driven than a simple threshold rule.

✅   **Core Concepts to Highlight**

* K-means clustering groups observations into a chosen number of clusters based on similarity.
* Rule-of-thumb segmentation uses analyst-chosen cutoffs, while K-means lets the grouping emerge from the data given the chosen variables and value of `k`.
* K-means is a distance-based method, so observations must be represented numerically.
* Scaling matters because variables with larger numerical ranges can otherwise dominate the distance calculation.
* The method is still not fully automatic: the analyst chooses the variables, the number of clusters, and how the data are prepared, while the algorithm carries out the cluster assignment and updating steps.

💬 **Suggested In-Class Prompts** (if needed)

* “What does it mean for two employees to be similar in a clustering problem?”
* “How is K-means more flexible than the rule-based segmentation from Exercise 2?”
* “Which parts of a K-means analysis are chosen by the analyst, and which parts are done by the algorithm?”
* “Why can’t K-means work directly with text labels such as department names?”
* “If one variable runs from 1 to 4 and another runs from 1,000 to 20,000, what might happen if we do not scale first?”
* “Does using a data-driven method remove the need for analyst judgment?”

📌 **Common Misunderstandings**

* Thinking K-means discovers the single true grouping in the data, rather than one grouping implied by the chosen variables and value of `k`
* Assuming K-means is completely objective while rule-of-thumb segmentation is completely subjective
* Thinking numeric variables are required because of software syntax rather than because the method uses numerical distances
* Believing scaling changes the meaning of the data, rather than putting variables on a comparable footing for the clustering step
* Assuming larger-numbered variables are automatically more important, when they may simply be measured on a bigger scale

:::

:::

:::

<!-- END PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-solutions -->

::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-solutions" when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.solution-block}

::: {.solution-block-header}
Solution
:::

::: {.solution-block-body}

**(a) Explain in your own words what K-means clustering is trying to achieve.**

K-means clustering divides observations into a chosen number of groups so that observations within the same group are similar to one another, while observations in different groups are less similar.

**For the analytically inclined:** A more detailed explanation is that K-means begins with a chosen number of cluster centres, called **centroids**. Each observation is assigned to the nearest centroid. The centroid of each cluster is then recalculated, and the process repeats until the assignments become stable.

**(b) Explain how K-means clustering differs from the rule-of-thumb segmentation you created in Exercise 2.**

Rule-of-thumb segmentation uses fixed cutoffs chosen by the analyst in advance. K-means clustering does not require the analyst to define the groups directly. Instead, it forms groups based on similarity in the data using the variables provided.

**(c) What is one advantage of using a rule-of-thumb segmentation instead of K-means clustering?**

A rule-of-thumb segmentation is easy to understand, explain, and communicate. Because the grouping rule is explicit, it is often easier to justify to non-technical audiences.

**(d) What is one advantage of using K-means clustering instead of a rule-of-thumb segmentation?**

K-means clustering can uncover patterns in the data that a simple hand-built rule may miss. It allows the grouping to be shaped by similarity across the variables rather than by fixed analyst-chosen cutoffs alone.

**(e) In K-means clustering, which parts of the analysis are chosen by the analyst, and which parts are carried out automatically by the algorithm?**

The analyst chooses which variables to include, how the data are prepared, and the number of clusters `k`. The algorithm then uses those inputs to assign observations to clusters and update the cluster centres until the grouping becomes stable.

**(f) Suppose an analytics team wants to use K-means clustering with variables such as `job_satisfaction`, `distance_from_home`, and `monthly_income`. Explain why it is important that these variables are numeric.**

K-means clustering works by comparing observations using numerical distance. To calculate how close or far apart two employees are, the method needs each observation to be represented as numbers. Numeric variables can be placed in a numerical feature space, which allows the algorithm to measure similarity across employees.

If a variable is stored as text or as a category label, K-means cannot directly interpret the difference between its values as numerical distance. That is why K-means is typically used with numeric variables, or with categorical variables that have first been converted into a suitable numeric form.

**(g) Explain why it is usually important to scale variables before running K-means clustering.**

Scaling is important because K-means is distance-based. If one variable has a much larger numerical range than another, it can dominate the distance calculation even if it is not conceptually more important. For example, `monthly_income` may vary by thousands of dollars, while `job_satisfaction` may vary only between 1 and 4.

Scaling puts variables onto a more comparable footing, so the clustering is driven more by relative patterns across variables rather than by whichever variable happens to be measured on the biggest scale.

:::

:::

:::

<!-- END PROFILE:r-solutions -->
